coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance criteria verifiability and completeness will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria verifiability and completeness

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.

verify command `grep -q 'git config user.name' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'git config user.email' AGENTS.md`.` greps the unanchored word 'git' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_git'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"git":')

verify command `grep -q 'git config user.name' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'git config user.email' AGENTS.md` greps the unanchored word 'git' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_git'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"git":')

verify command `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -q "configured git identity" "$f" || exit 1; done` exits 0 (each file must contain the phrase, or the loop exits non-zero on the first miss), AND `git grep -l 'Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md'` returns no results.` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote

verify command `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -q "configured git identity" "$f" || exit 1; done` has an unquoted shell variable ($VAR/$?) in its grep — it expands before grep runs; the pattern is not what you wrote
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC3's bare `git grep` verify with a pipeline that self-asserts the 'only allowed prefixes' constraint, e.g.: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' | grep -vE '^(infra|.github/workflows|tests|docs/experiments)/' | grep -qv .` (exits non-zero when no disallowed matches exist, non-zero exits 1 when a disallowed match is found — invert with `! pipeline` as appropriate).
evidence: "Verify: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'` returns matches ONLY under `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, or `docs/experiments/`; any match outside those prefixes fails the check."
ABSENCE: no pipeline assertion (grep -v / awk / shell conditional) that translates 'only under allowed prefixes' into a non-zero exit code when a match appears outside those prefixes.
findings.finding: AC3's verify command (`git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'`) lists all matches across the entire repo but does NOT self-assert the 'ONLY under allowed prefixes' constraint — the executor must manually inspect output and judge whether every match falls under infra/, .github/workflows/, tests/, or docs/experiments/. This is a verify-command defect (taxonomy item 6: prose/human-inspection target instead of structured assertion): the command can exit 0 with zero matches OR with matches only in allowed paths, and it can return matches outside allowed paths with a non-zero exit only if the pattern is absent everywhere — but 'returns matches ONLY under' is never enforced by the bare git grep invocation.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC3 ("Verify: `git grep -nE ...` returns matches ONLY under ...")
scenarios: A future commit adds Joe Oakhart's identity to docs/design/contributing-guide.md; the bare git grep exits 0 (because it does find matches, not because they're all allowed) and the AC is silently satisfied while violating the constraint.
An executor runs the command, sees a match, but cannot tell from the command's exit code alone whether the match is in an allowed path.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC3 verify with:
`! git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' | grep -vE '^(infra/|\.github/workflows/|tests/|docs/experiments/)'`
This pipes all matches through grep -v to strip allowed-path lines; if any disallowed-path line remains, the overall pipeline exits 0 on the inner grep (meaning a bad match exists), and the leading `!` inverts it to fail the check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Remediation whether CONTRIBUTING.md needs to be edited to contain the literal phrase 'configured git identity', or adjust AC4's loop to exclude CONTRIBUTING.md if its existing wording is already acceptable.
evidence: "CONTRIBUTING.md:198-214 requires a real contributor name and email for DCO sign-off" — presented as already-correct evidence in the Finding.

"Make the canonical guidance identity-neutral: contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor... Retain Joe Oakhart's bot identity only in narrowly scoped automation guidance" — remediation targets AGENTS.md and .agents/rules/*.md; CONTRIBUTING.md edits are not mentioned.

AC4 prove loop: `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -q "configured git identity" "$f" || exit 1; done` — requires the exact phrase in all three files including CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.finding: AC4 requires the exact phrase 'configured git identity' to appear verbatim in CONTRIBUTING.md (`for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -q "configured git identity" "$f" || exit 1; done`), but the Remediation section does not explicitly task editing CONTRIBUTING.md — it describes CONTRIBUTING.md as already-correct evidence (the Finding cites it to show the current DCO policy) and scopes the rewrite work to AGENTS.md and .agents/rules/*.md. An executor following the remediation steps may leave CONTRIBUTING.md untouched, causing AC4's loop to exit non-zero on the first iteration.
findings.location: Remediation section + Acceptance Criteria AC4
scenarios: Executor reads the Finding, concludes CONTRIBUTING.md is already compliant, skips it, and AC4 fails because the exact phrase 'configured git identity' was never added.
Executor adds the phrase only to AGENTS.md and rebar.md, PR CI passes on those two but fails on CONTRIBUTING.md.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Remediation: 'Also update CONTRIBUTING.md:198–214 to include the phrase "configured git identity (`git config user.name`/`user.email`)" so all three canonical files pass the AC4 consistency loop.' Or, if CONTRIBUTING.md's current wording is deemed sufficient, change AC4's loop to check only AGENTS.md and .agents/rules/rebar.md and use a separate, wording-flexible check for CONTRIBUTING.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete negative-path proving command to AC5, e.g.: `cp AGENTS.md /tmp/AGENTS.md.bak && echo 'Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart <joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com>' >> AGENTS.md && make lint; EXIT=$?; mv /tmp/AGENTS.md.bak AGENTS.md; [ $EXIT -ne 0 ]` — so the executor can deterministically verify the guard fires.
evidence: "running it against a scratch copy with the hardcoded string reinjected exits non-zero" — describes outcome but provides no command to create the scratch state or invoke the check against it.
ABSENCE: no `sed`/`echo` + `make lint` command sequence for the negative-path test.
findings.finding: AC5's negative-path verification ('running it against a scratch copy with the hardcoded string reinjected exits non-zero') describes the desired behavior in prose but provides no concrete proving command — no `echo`/`sed` invocation to create the scratch copy, no specific command to run the lint target against it, and no assertion on exit code. An executor must invent the test scaffolding from scratch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5 ("Verify: the new check's script/target exists, running it against the post-change guidance files exits 0, and running it against a scratch copy with the hardcoded string reinjected exits non-zero.")
scenarios: An executor writes a lint check that always exits 0 (e.g. empty script); the positive-path test passes trivially; without a concrete negative-path command, the executor has no deterministic way to verify the check actually fails on reinjection.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC5's Verify block: 'Negative-path test: `cp AGENTS.md /tmp/agents_bak.md && printf "\nSigned-off-by: Joe Oakhart <joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com>\n" >> AGENTS.md && make lint; rc=$?; mv /tmp/agents_bak.md AGENTS.md; test $rc -ne 0` — the final `test $rc -ne 0` must exit 0 to confirm the guard fires.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Remediation carve-out list with AC #3's allowlist: either add `CLAUDE.local.md` to AC #3's allowed prefixes (and to the lint check exclusion), or remove it from the Remediation's example list so both sections enumerate the same permitted locations.
evidence: Remediation: 'Retain Joe Oakhart's bot identity only in narrowly scoped automation guidance for a workflow that actually runs as that bot (e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`)'

AC #3: 'git grep … returns matches ONLY under `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, or `docs/experiments/`; any match outside those prefixes fails the check' — `CLAUDE.local.md` is not in this list
findings.finding: The Remediation section names `CLAUDE.local.md` as a valid location to retain the Joe Oakhart bot identity ('narrowly scoped automation guidance … e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`'), but AC #3's allowlist omits `CLAUDE.local.md` — it permits the identity only under `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, or `docs/experiments/`. An implementer who follows the Remediation and places scoped automation guidance in `CLAUDE.local.md` will fail the AC #3 verify check, while an implementer who follows AC #3 strictly will produce a file that contradicts the Remediation's stated carve-out.
findings.location: Remediation paragraph 1 vs. AC #3 verify step
scenarios: Implementer adds scoped bot identity to CLAUDE.local.md per Remediation guidance; AC #3 grep finds it and fails the check.
Implementer removes bot identity from CLAUDE.local.md to satisfy AC #3; Remediation's carve-out is silently violated.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `CLAUDE.local.md` to AC #3's allowlist: change 'ONLY under `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, or `docs/experiments/`' to 'ONLY under `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, `docs/experiments/`, or `CLAUDE.local.md`', and also exclude `CLAUDE.local.md` from the lint check pathspecs in AC #5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC #5) that verifies the inline documentation of intentional exclusions exists, e.g. `grep -q 'intentional exclusion\|infra.*excluded\|docs/experiments' <script-or-Makefile-target>`.
evidence: Remediation: 'Document, next to the check, which file owns it and that `infra/`/`.github/workflows/`/`tests/`/`docs/experiments/` are the intentional exclusions.'
AC #5 verify steps: check script/target exists, exits 0 on clean files, exits non-zero on reinjected string — no step checks for the documentation comment.
findings.finding: The Remediation requires the lint check to be accompanied by inline documentation ('Document, next to the check, which file owns it and that `infra/`/`.github/workflows/`/`tests/`/`docs/experiments/` are the intentional exclusions'), but no AC verifies that this documentation exists. The deliverable is described but has no covering criterion.
findings.location: Remediation final paragraph vs. AC #5
scenarios: Implementer ships a valid lint script with no inline documentation of exclusions; all ACs pass but the described deliverable is incomplete and future maintainers have no in-code explanation of why certain paths are exempt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either mandate the exact phrase 'configured git identity' in the Remediation's prose so implementers know to include it, or replace the AC #4 grep with a more robust check (e.g. `grep -qE 'git config user\.(name|email)'`) that verifies the instructional content rather than a specific phrasing.
evidence: AC #4: 'grep -q "configured git identity" "$f" || exit 1' — requires exact phrase in all three files

Remediation: 'contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit (`git config user.name` / `git config user.email`)' — does not mandate the phrase 'configured git identity'
findings.finding: AC #4's verify step requires the literal phrase 'configured git identity' to appear in each of `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.agents/rules/rebar.md`, but the Remediation only says the guidance should describe the source as 'the committer's own configured git identity (`git config user.name`/`user.email`)' — it does not explicitly instruct authors to use that exact phrase. An implementer who paraphrases (e.g. 'use your own git-configured name and email') will produce correct-intent guidance that fails AC #4's grep.
findings.location: AC #4 verify step
scenarios: Implementer writes 'use the name and email from your git configuration' in AGENTS.md — semantically correct, but the AC #4 grep exits non-zero, falsely reporting a failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC #4's verify loop with: `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -qE 'git config user\.(name|email)' "$f" || exit 1; done` — this checks for the substantive instruction without requiring a specific phrase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Externalize the forbidden-identity pattern (e.g., as a shell variable or a one-line config file such as `scripts/dco-lint-pattern.txt`) so that the script, Makefile target, and any prose referencing the exact regex all derive from a single source of truth that is easy to update when bot identities change.
evidence: Plan specifies the literal regex `joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart` inline in both the Makefile/script description and AC5 without mentioning a single source-of-truth constant or config location.
T5e sub-check (b): 'Rules/thresholds expected to evolve are configurable, not hardcoded.' Bot identities can change as automation evolves.
findings.finding: The forbidden-identity grep pattern (`joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart`) is hardcoded directly into the lint script/Makefile target with no mechanism for extension or central configuration. If the bot identity changes or additional bot identities are onboarded, the pattern must be located and updated manually in the script, the Makefile, and potentially the ACs.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Add a deterministic consistency check' paragraph; AC5
scenarios: Bot email address changes (e.g., domain migration); pattern in script no longer matches real bot identity, lint silently allows the old pattern through.
A second automation bot is added; maintainer must remember to add its identity to the pattern in multiple places (script AND ACs AND any docs referencing the exact grep command).
findings.suggested_fix: In the lint script, define the pattern as a shell variable at the top (e.g., `FORBIDDEN_DCO_PATTERN='joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'`) and reference `$FORBIDDEN_DCO_PATTERN` in the `git grep` invocation. Note the variable name and its location in the plan's documentation requirement so that AC5's prose can point to the variable rather than duplicating the literal regex.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace single-quoted pathspec magic in the `make lint` git-grep command with a form that works on both POSIX shells and Windows (e.g. use a shell script with `git grep` and double-quoted arguments, or document that the target requires Git Bash/MSYS2 on Windows, or use a Python/cross-platform script instead of a bare `git grep` invocation).
evidence: Plan prescribes: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob)docs/**/*.md' ':(exclude)docs/experiments/**'` — single-quoted POSIX pathspec magic
Supported matrix cell: Windows developer workstations

On Windows cmd.exe, single quotes are not string delimiters; they are passed as literal characters to child processes, so git receives `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` (with quote chars) rather than `:(glob)docs/**/*.md`
Git pathspec magic requires the `:(` prefix without embedded single-quote characters; git will error with 'invalid pathspec magic'
findings.finding: The `make lint`-integrated `git grep` command uses POSIX single-quote syntax for pathspec magic arguments (`':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` and `':(exclude)docs/experiments/**'`). On Windows without a POSIX-compatible shell (i.e., cmd.exe or native PowerShell), `make` invokes cmd.exe as the shell; single-quote characters are passed literally to git.exe, which does not recognise `':(glob)...'` (with embedded quotes) as valid pathspec magic, causing git to error or silently treat the argument as a literal path.
findings.location: Remediation section and AC5 — `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob)docs/**/*.md' ':(exclude)docs/experiments/**'`
scenarios: Windows developer workstation running `make lint` via cmd.exe: git grep exits with `fatal: invalid pathspec ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'`, causing the lint target to fail for the wrong reason (quoting error, not a detected hardcoded identity), or the check is silently skipped if the Makefile ignores the exit code — either way the regression guard does not function as designed on Windows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting the plan into (a) a doc-only ticket for the identity-neutral sweep (ACs #1–#4) and (b) a separate CI-hygiene ticket for the make-lint grep check (AC #5), since each delivers independent value and has a distinct reason to change.
evidence: ACs #1–#4 are satisfied entirely by doc edits to AGENTS.md, .agents/rules/rebar.md, CONTRIBUTING.md
AC #5 introduces a new Makefile target or script under scripts/, integrated into make lint — a distinct engineering artifact with its own lifecycle, not needed for the doc fix to deliver value
The Remediation section separates the two concerns: 'Make the canonical guidance identity-neutral' (doc concern) and 'Add a deterministic consistency check' (CI concern)
findings.finding: The plan bundles two independently-valuable, independently-releasable outcomes: (1) a pure documentation sweep making contributor guidance identity-neutral, and (2) introducing new build-enforcement infrastructure (a make-lint-integrated grep script). These have distinct reasons to change — doc accuracy concerns vs. CI/build engineering — and each delivers standalone value.
findings.location: Plan overall; Remediation section (grep script spec) and AC #5 vs. ACs #1–#4
scenarios: A team that already has lint infrastructure in flux might want to ship the doc fix immediately while deferring the lint check to a separate CI hygiene ticket
A reviewer wanting to merge the doc fix quickly is blocked by lint-script implementation details that are orthogonal to the doc correctness
If the lint check design changes (e.g. migrating from Makefile to a pre-commit hook), the doc fix ticket must be reopened for an unrelated reason

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC3 and AC5: either remove `CLAUDE.local.md` from AC3's acceptable-retention list (since it's gitignored and git grep can't see it, no exclusion is needed), or add a note in AC5 explaining that `CLAUDE.local.md` is safe to omit from the exclusion list because it is gitignored.
evidence: AC3 text: 'Any retained Joe-specific identity is explicitly scoped to the automation that owns it (e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`)'
AC5 text lists exclusions: 'infra/, .github/workflows/, tests/ are excluded by not being passed as pathspecs' — CLAUDE.local.md is absent from this list
.gitignore:38 confirms CLAUDE.local.md is gitignored (so git grep will never find it, making the omission harmless in practice but confusing in the spec)
findings.finding: AC3's prose names `CLAUDE.local.md` as an acceptable location to retain the bot identity, but AC5's lint grep exclusion list omits it — creating an implementer-confusing inconsistency. Although `CLAUDE.local.md` is gitignored (so git grep cannot surface it), an implementer following AC3 literally might retain the bot identity there, then find AC5's exclusion logic does not cover that case and seek clarification.
findings.location: AC3 prose ('e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`') vs AC5 exclusion list ('infra/', '.github/workflows/', 'tests/', 'docs/experiments/')
scenarios: An implementer reads AC3, retains bot identity in CLAUDE.local.md, then reads AC5 and wonders whether the lint check would falsely fire on that file (it can't, since git ignores it, but this is non-obvious).
A future change that un-gitignores CLAUDE.local.md would immediately cause the AC5 lint check to fire with no exclusion path.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace the parenthetical with '(e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`)' — dropping `CLAUDE.local.md` since it is gitignored and invisible to git grep, making it irrelevant to the lint check. Add a one-line comment in AC5: 'Note: CLAUDE.local.md is gitignored and therefore invisible to git grep regardless of its contents.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC4's verify command with the actual string the Remediation instructs implementers to write: either change the `grep -q "configured git identity"` probe to `grep -q 'git config user.name'` (matching what AC1 already tests), or add an explicit instruction in the Remediation section telling each of the three files to include the exact phrase `configured git identity`.
evidence: AC1 verify: `grep -q 'git config user.name' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'git config user.email' AGENTS.md` — anchored to the actual instruction text the plan mandates
AC4 verify loop requires the phrase 'configured git identity' — a specific string that appears nowhere in the Remediation section's instructed content

Remediation says: 'contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit (git config user.name / git config user.email)' — no mandate to write 'configured git identity' as a literal phrase
The AC4 description also says 'all three name "the committer's own configured git identity (git config user.name/user.email)"' — this phrasing is aspirational prose, not a verbatim string instruction
findings.finding: AC4's verify command requires the literal phrase 'configured git identity' to appear verbatim in CONTRIBUTING.md, AGENTS.md, and .agents/rules/rebar.md, but the Remediation section never instructs the implementer to insert that exact phrase — it only specifies adding references to 'git config user.name'/'git config user.email'. An implementer who correctly follows the plan will produce files that pass AC1's grep but fail AC4's loop on first miss, with no prescribed fix.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 verify command: `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -q "configured git identity" "$f" || exit 1; done`
scenarios: Implementer adds 'verify git config user.name and user.email and sign off with that configured real identity' to all three files — correct per Remediation — but AC4's loop exits 1 on the first file, falsely signalling failure.
Implementer tries to satisfy both AC1 and AC4 but must guess which exact phrase to embed in all three files.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC4 verify loop with one that checks for strings actually mandated by the Remediation: `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -qE 'git config user\.(name|email)' "$f" || exit 1; done` — this is consistent with AC1's approach and with what the plan instructs implementers to write.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove `CLAUDE.local.md` from the Remediation's list of scoped-exception locations (it is git-ignored and therefore invisible to `git grep` by construction, requiring no explicit pathspec exclusion), or add a parenthetical clarifying that it is auto-excluded because it is git-ignored and untracked.
evidence: .gitignore:38: `CLAUDE.local.md` — confirmed git-ignored
CLAUDE.md:15: 'Machine/operator-local settings … live in the git-ignored CLAUDE.local.md' — the file is intentionally excluded from version control
AC5 grep command does not include CLAUDE.local.md as a scanned or excluded pathspec — it is simply absent
AC3 verify: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'` — git grep does not scan untracked/ignored files, so CLAUDE.local.md matches are silently skipped
findings.finding: The plan lists CLAUDE.local.md as an acceptable location to retain the bot identity ('e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`'), but CLAUDE.local.md is git-ignored (.gitignore:38) and therefore structurally invisible to git grep. The plan never acknowledges this, leaving ambiguity about whether CLAUDE.local.md is 'safe' because git grep can't see it, or whether it requires separate manual auditing. The AC3 verify step (`git grep -nE ...` across the full repo) also silently skips git-ignored files.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Retain Joe Oakhart's bot identity only in narrowly scoped automation guidance … e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`'
scenarios: A future operator adds bot identity to CLAUDE.local.md (the plan says it's fine); the lint check passes silently because git grep can't see ignored files; the policy becomes untestable at that location.
An implementer reading the plan tries to reason about whether CLAUDE.local.md needs to be audited and finds no guidance, wasting time on a non-issue.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation section, change 'e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`' to 'e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`' with a note: '(`CLAUDE.local.md` is git-ignored and therefore invisible to `git grep` by construction — no explicit exclusion is needed).' This eliminates the implied-but-unverifiable exception.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback/error-handling rule to the updated AGENTS.md (and mirrored in .agents/rules/rebar.md) specifying what an agent or contributor should do when `git config user.name` or `git config user.email` returns empty — e.g., 'if either is unset, configure them explicitly before committing: `git config user.name "Your Name" && git config user.email "you@example.com"`' — so the processing rule is complete for all environment states.
evidence: Plan Remediation: 'contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit (git config user.name / git config user.email)'
AC1: 'AGENTS.md tells contributors and agents to verify git config user.name and git config user.email and to sign off with that configured real identity'
No fallback specified anywhere in the plan for the case where git config user.name or user.email returns empty or exits non-zero

Prior review cache corroborates: .rebar/cache/plan-review/35d2-8cd6-161c-4e7f/b208740adcc07ed3710d.json: 'The plan instructs agents to sign off using git config user.name / git config user.email but specifies no fallback or decision rule for the common agent-environment case where these values are not configured'
Agent environments (CI runners, fresh worktrees) commonly lack pre-configured git identity, making this a real failure mode
findings.finding: The plan instructs agents to sign off using the identity from 'git config user.name' / 'git config user.email', but specifies no fallback or decision rule for the agent-environment case where one or both of these values are unconfigured (empty string or absent). This is a processing-protocol gap in an agent-facing rule file: an agent executing the rule in a fresh CI environment or a minimally configured worktree may encounter an empty sign-off line or a git error, with no specified recovery action.
findings.location: Remediation section (processing rule for agents); AC1 (AGENTS.md acceptance criterion); .agents/rules/rebar.md
scenarios: An LLM agent running in a CI-spawned environment where git user.name/user.email are not pre-configured runs 'git commit -s' and produces a malformed or empty Signed-off-by trailer, then pushes to Gerrit — which rejects the commit without the agent knowing what corrective action to take.
A human contributor sets up a fresh worktree following AGENTS.md guidance, has no global git identity configured, and the guidance gives no error-recovery path.
findings.suggested_fix: In the updated AGENTS.md sign-off section, after instructing agents to verify git config user.name/user.email, add: 'If either value is empty or unset, configure your identity before committing: `git config user.name "<Your Name>" && git config user.email "<you@example.com>"`. A commit with an empty Signed-off-by trailer will be rejected by Gerrit at push time.'

findings.finding: AC4's proving command (`grep -c ... | reports at least one match in each of the three files`) does not fail automatically when a file has zero matches — `grep -c` with multiple files prints per-file counts and exits 0 even when one file shows `filename:0`, so the command cannot serve as a binary gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 verify command: `grep -c "committer's own configured git identity\|configured git identity" CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md`
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do
  grep -qE "committer's own configured git identity|configured git identity" "$f" \
    || { echo "FAIL: $f missing required phrase"; exit 1; }
done
```

findings.finding: AC3's proving command (`git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'`) lists all matches repo-wide but provides no mechanism to fail when a match appears outside the allowed prefixes — an executor must manually inspect output and judge, making the check non-deterministic.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 verify command: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'` … returns matches ONLY under allowed prefixes
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' \
  | grep -vE '^(infra/|\.github/workflows/|tests/|docs/experiments/)' \
  | { read line && echo "FAIL: out-of-scope match: $line" && exit 1 || exit 0; }
```

findings.finding: The plan asserts that the AC5 consistency-check grep pathspec `'docs/**/*.md'` is recursive and 'also covers docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/, etc.' but git grep does NOT expand `**` as a recursive glob by default — a bare `'docs/**/*.md'` pathspec matches only files named `*.md` in an immediate subdirectory of docs/ (i.e., `docs/X/*.md`), not deeper nesting, and on many git versions matches nothing at all. The docs/ directory is confirmed to have the named subdirectories (adr/, design/, migrations/, calibration/, research/, experiments/, etc.), so the claimed recursive coverage is empirically false for the prescribed command.
findings.location: Remediation section (consistency-check grep command) and AC5 verify command: `git grep -nE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'`
findings.suggested_fix:
Use `git grep -rn --include='*.md'` (dropping the pathspec glob in favor of `-r` + `--include`) or use the magic pathspec `:(glob)docs/**/*.md`. Example corrected command:
```
git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' \
  -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'
```
Alternatively, invoke `grep -r --include='*.md'` over `docs/` as a standard shell command rather than a git pathspec.

findings.finding: The AC5 lint grep command uses the unquoted-glob pathspec 'docs/**/*.md' passed to git grep. Without the :(glob) pathspec magic prefix, git grep does not guarantee recursive ** expansion across all git versions — it may silently match only files directly under docs/ rather than the subdirectories the plan explicitly names (docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/). The plan asserts recursive coverage of these subdirectories in the same sentence, creating a mismatch between stated scope and the literal command.
findings.location: Remediation section and AC5 (the git grep command: `git grep -nE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'`)
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace `'docs/**/*.md'` with `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` in both the Remediation grep command and the AC5 verify command, e.g.:
```
git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' \
  -- AGENTS.md ':(glob).agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'
```
This uses git's built-in glob pathspec magic, which guarantees recursive `**` semantics across all git versions ≥ 1.9.

findings.finding: The lint check mechanism has an unresolved exclusion gap: AC5 specifies scanning `docs/**/*.md` (recursive) while simultaneously listing `docs/experiments/` as an intentional exclusion, but never specifies the `git grep` syntax to implement that exclusion. The `docs/experiments/` tree contains hundreds of `joeoakhart` hits (corpus JSONL, harness scripts with `/Users/joeoakhart/` paths, eval files referencing the bot email). A naive implementation of the prescribed grep template — without a `:!docs/experiments/` negative pathspec or equivalent — will permanently fail the lint gate, making the consistency check unusable.
findings.location: Remediation section (lint check grep command) and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Update the grep template in both the Remediation section and AC5 to: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md' ':!docs/experiments/**'`

findings.finding: The plan prescribes `git grep -nE ... -- 'docs/**/*.md'` and explicitly asserts this pathspec recursively covers `docs/adr/`, `docs/design/`, `docs/migrations/`, `docs/calibration/`, etc., but `git grep` does NOT treat `**` as a recursive glob without the `:(glob)` pathspec magic prefix; without it, the pathspec may silently fail to match files in subdirectories on many git versions, leaving the consistency check incomplete.
findings.location: Remediation section (git grep command) and AC5 verify step
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `'docs/**/*.md'` with `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` in the git grep command, which explicitly enables pathspec glob magic and guarantees recursive subdirectory matching regardless of git version. Alternatively, use `$(git ls-files 'docs/**/*.md')` or a `find docs/ -name '*.md'` pipe into `xargs grep` to achieve portable recursive coverage.

findings.finding: AC4 mandates a specific literal phrase ('committer's own configured git identity') in CONTRIBUTING.md and the other two files via grep, but the remediation section only says to 'describe the concept' — an executor who writes semantically correct but differently worded guidance will satisfy the intent yet fail the proving command, creating an unresolvable conflict.
findings.location: Remediation section ('Make the canonical guidance identity-neutral') vs. AC4 verify command
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Remediation section: 'When updating the files, include the sentence: "Sign off using the committer's own configured git identity (`git config user.name` / `git config user.email`)."' — then the AC4 grep pattern matches deterministically.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
